question: A FEEL output mapping evaluates the indexed list access below. What value is returned?

options.text: An incident is raised because the index is out of range.

a.text: FEEL list indices start at `1`, so `0` is out of range. The docs explicitly show `[1,2,3,4][0]` evaluating to `null` rather than to the first element.

b.text: Returning `1` would require zero-based indexing, but FEEL is one-based; the first element is at index `1`, not `0`.

c.text: Negative wrap-around starts at `-1` for the last element. The literal index `0` does not wrap to the last position.

d.text: Out-of-range indices return `null` rather than raising an incident; this is the same behaviour as accessing index `5` or `-5`.

explanation: FEEL list indices begin at `1`, and indices outside the range of the list return `null`. The docs include `[1,2,3,4][0]` as an explicit example evaluating to `null`. This one-based convention is a well-known gotcha for developers coming from zero-indexed languages.
